paper.title: Learnable Fourier Features for Multi-dimensional Spatial Positional Encoding
paper.abstract: Attentional mechanisms are order-invariant. Positional encoding is a crucial component to allow attention-based deep model architectures such as Transformer to address sequences or images where the position of information matters. In this paper, we propose a novel positional encoding method based on learnable Fourier features. Instead of hard-coding each position as a token or a vector, we represent each position, which can be multi-dimensional, as a trainable encoding based on learnable Fourier feature mapping, modulated  with  a  multi-layer perceptron. The representation is particularly advantageous for a spatial multi-dimensional position, e.g., pixel positions on an image, where $L_2$ distances or more complex positional relationships need to be captured. Our experiments based on several public benchmark tasks show that our learnable Fourier feature representation for multi-dimensional positional encoding outperforms existing methods by both improving the accuracy and allowing faster convergence.

review_fields.summary: The paper presents a new positional encoding method for Transformer-like architectures. Different from the previous methods that learn embeddings vectors for each position, the proposal instead learns an encoding function based on a set of Fourier features. The method by design learns an inductive bias and is parameter-efficient. While simple, the proposal outperforms the previous methods on a range of vision tasks.
review_fields.main_review:
[Originality]
- The idea of using the Fourier feature to approximate shift-invariant Gaussian kernel is not new [24,25]. However, the authors re-cast this basic idea to learn the position encoding, since the Gaussian kernel can encode euclidean distance between two points essentially. This is new in that previous position encoding methods learn positon-wise embedding vectors directly, which suffers from training and testing inconsistency due to the supervision sparsity.

[Quality]
- The proposal is well ablated and compared with various baselines in the experiments.
- Extensive experiments on various types of vision tasks and transformer models demonstrate the proposal's generality.

[Clarity]
- The motivation of the paper is well illustrated with several figures. 
- The description of the method is well written and its implementation details are well-summarized in the algorithm box.

[Significance]
- The positional encoding method in Transformer-architectures has rarely been thoroughly studied. The proposal shows the drawback of the previous methods and presents a new way of encoding positions in Transformer using Fourier features. While simple, it consistently outperforms the previous approaches on various vision tasks. The results should be shared with the community to rethink the current position encoding methods.

[Suggestion]
- Please compare the positional similarity visualization with the previous approaches. We can then see at what point the proposal performs better than the previous approaches. Also, please discuss what type of errors still exist in the current proposal.
- Please also show some qualitative results of the learned positional similarity along with the input image. This might be more intuitive for the reader. Moreover, if possible, please show how the well-learned positional similarity also positively affects the content-matching as well.
